title: Coffee with Cream on Wool (Combination Stain)
stainChemistry: Coffee-cream on wool is challenging: cream proteins bond to wool's own protein structure, and we cannot use Protein enzymes (they'd digest wool too). Tannins also bond. Wool requires cold treatment (no heat triggers felting), no enzymes, and careful sequencing. NSD for protein component, then acid for combination tannin (from coffee) and oil (from cream)—but gentler than any other fiber type.
whyThisWorks: Cold water removes proteins without enzyme help. NSD suspends residual casein/whey without alkalinity. Acetic acid breaks remaining tannin bonds. Wool's delicate nature demands the gentlest possible chemistry, but the sequence still works: protein first, tannin second, extreme caution throughout.

spottingProtocol.instruction: cold water from back. Blot hard. Salt helps. Flipping and continuing. Work concentric circles from edge inward. Never rub—wool felts. Most fresh casein/whey will transfer to cloth in the first 1-2 minutes if you work immediately.

Sponge gently onto stain. Detergent encapsulates remaining casein/whey. After 2-3 minutes, blot gently—downward pressure only, no rubbing. Stain lightens as proteins suspend. This is the protein removal stage.
Rinse completely in cold water. NSD left behind will interfere with acid treatment. Work from back to front. Water must run absolutely clear with no bubbles.

Now apply acidity for tannin removal. Mix vinegar 1:1 with cold water. Apply in small droplets. After 1 minute, blot. Don't overdo—wool weakens with prolonged acid exposure. Brown coffee color should fade visibly.
If brown remains, dilute 3% peroxide 1:1 with cold water. Sponge gently. Wool is protein—dilute peroxide is safer. After 1-2 minutes, blot. Peroxide oxidizes tannin pigment.
Rinse thoroughly. All acid and peroxide must be removed. Lay flat, work back to front.

homeSolutions: Test on a hidden area first (inside seam or hem) — wool dyes may react to cleaning agents.
FRESH: Immediately ice water + blot from back. Most casein comes out with this step.
REMAINING: dish-soap solution (1:9 with cold water), 2 minutes, blot, rinse thoroughly.
BROWN COLOR: white vinegar (1:1 diluted), 1 minute only, blot. Then diluted hydrogen peroxide 3% from the pharmacy (1 part peroxide with 1 part cold water), 1-2 minutes, blot, rinse thoroughly.
materialWarnings: ALWAYS test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — wool dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
escalation: If combo stain persists after NSD + acid + peroxide treatment, call professional. Wool-safe enzymes (inhibitor-formulation) or solvent cleaning may help, but risk is high.

household.name: Wool-Safe Detergent (pH-neutral, enzyme-free)
household.note: Enzyme-free, pH-neutral wool wash. Household substitute for professional NSD. Gentle detergent for protein. 10% in cold water. Use first.

household.name: White Vinegar
household.note: For tannin. Dilute 1:1 with cold water. 1 minute max. DO NOT substitute industrial-strength (28%) acetic acid — it causes chemical burns.

professional.name: Mulsolite
professional.note: Professional NSD for wool protein removal.

customerExplanation: Coffee-cream on wool requires careful two-stage treatment: detergent for protein, acid for tannin. Wool is delicate—no heat, no enzymes, no agitation. We must work cold and gentle throughout.
commonMistakes: Using warm water—causes felting AND sets protein
Using enzymes—people assume enzymes help. Protein damages wool's own protein.
Treating tannin before protein—acid causes casein to set deeper if still present
scienceNote: Wool contains keratin protein bonded with disulfide bridges and hydrogen bonds. Casein (milk protein) is a globular protein that bonds to keratin through similar forces. When heated (above 30°C), these bonds strengthen and water-activated disulfide cross-linking accelerates (felting). This is why heat is forbidden on wool stains. NSD works through mild detergency—it doesn't break disulfide bridges the way heat does.
callPro: If combo stain is older than 6-8 hours or if treatment doesn't fully remove color, call professional wool specialist. Risk of damage increases with extended treatment.
solventNote: Professional: Cold NSD (Mulsolite) soak (2-3 min), sponge work, thorough rinse. Then acetic acid spotter (GAA diluted 1:10, cold, 1-2 min max). For brown tannin, dilute H₂O₂ (1.5-2%, cold, 2 min). For aged stains, reducing bleach (StreePene 0.25%, cold, max 5 min) then rinse thoroughly. Temperature critical: never exceed 30°C. VDS post-treatment optional.
aftercare: Squeeze gently in towel—never wring. Lay flat on clean dry towel. Never use heat. Wool must dry slowly and evenly.
